config.ranking_rule: mean primary similarity; disqualify mean WER > 0.15 or any synthesis failure; tie-break WER then RTF

samples.transcript: You cannot wish a technology away, artificial intelligence will reshape work, whether we like it or not. The question for Singapore is whether our people are trained to use it or displaced by those who are.
Social media rewards the loudest voice, not the soundest argument. I would not ban it. I would make sure our schools teach young people to think for themselves before the algorithm thinks for them.
The contest between America and China will define this century. Small states like Singapore do not choose sides. We make ourselves useful to both and indispensable to neither's enemies.
Cryptocurrency, I am skeptical. A currency must rest on something. Production, trust, the credit of a state. Speculation is not an economy. But the technology beneath it may yet prove useful.
Working from home suits some trades and not others. What matters is output, not attendance. But a society that never gathers loses something. Discipline, cohesion, the habit of common purpose.

Water taught Singapore that survival is an engineering problem. We built new water because dependence is vulnerability. Climate change is the same lesson on a larger scale. Adapt early or plead later.
In August 1965, we were expelled from Malaysia with no hinterland, no resources, and two million people to feed. We did not waste time on grievance. We got on with the job.

Bilingualism was among my hardest policies. English for the words business, the mother tongue. Mandarin, Malay, Tamil, for one's roots. I pushed Mandarin over Hokkien and the other dialects and I would do it again.
When we started, most of our people lived in squatterhuts and campongs. The HDB, funded through the CPF, gave over 80% of Singaporeans a home they own. A man who owns his flat defends his country.

ASEAN works precisely because it moves slowly, Indonesia, Malaysia, Thailand, Vietnam, different systems, different histories. Consensus is frustrating, but it has kept the peace in Southeast Asia for two generations.
That is, outside anything I can judge with confidence. I never had to decide such a question, and I will not pretend otherwise. I can tell you the principles I would apply. Nothing more.

If you ask me what made the difference between the countries that prospered after the war and the countries that stagnated, I will tell you it was not resources, nor size, nor the slogans of their leaders, but the quality of their institutions, the discipline of their people, and the honesty of their governments. And all three must hold at once, because any one of them failing will unravel the other two.

The test of a policy is never, whether it sounds compassionate in a speech or wins applause at an election rally, but whether, 10 or 20 years on, the ordinary citizen, the man driving the taxi, the woman raising children in a three-room flat, is measurably better off than he or she would otherwise have been.
I stepped down as Prime Minister in November 1990 after 31 years. By then, our per capita income had risen from about $500 to over $12,000. Numbers are not everything, but they do not lie.
Mayor Dicker meant freedom, but freedom to do what? A flag and an anthem do not feed anyone. Independence is the beginning of the examination, not the end of it.
Singaporeans call it Kyasu, afraid to lose. In moderation it is drive, in excess it is paralysis. I prefer the people hungry to win over a people terrified of failing.
Deng Xiaoping was the ablest leader I ever met. He saw what Singapore had done, drew his own conclusions and turned China around. One man at the right moment can bend history.
Meritocracy is not a slogan, it is a discipline. The moment you appoint a man for his connections, rather than his ability, you have told every able man in the system that effort is pointless.
No, that is populism and populism is expensive. Somebody always pays, the politician merely arranges for the bill to arrive after he has left office.
I do not expect to be loved for what I did. I expect it to be judged by results. Singapore works. That is my answer. And history can make of it what it will.

You cannot wish a technology away. Artificial intelligence will reshape work whether we like it or not. The question for Singapore is whether our people are trained to use it or displaced by those who are.
